This package provides handy routines for dealing with text, such as
wrapping, substitution, trimming, stripping, prefix and suffix removal,
line continuation, indentation, comment processing, identifier processing,
values parsing, case insensitive comparison, and more. See the docs
(linked in the badge above) for the detailed documentation and examples.

# Layouts

One of the features of this package is the layouts module, which
provides a simple example of translating keystrokes from one keyboard
layout to another:

```
echo qwerty | python -m jaraco.text.to-dvorak
',.pyf
echo  "',.pyf" | python -m jaraco.text.to-qwerty
qwerty
```

# Newline Reporting

Need to know what newlines appear in a file?

```
$ python -m jaraco.text.show-newlines README.rst
newline is '\n'
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.layouts.translate
documentation:
```pycon
>>> translate('dvorak', to_dvorak)
'ekrpat'
>>> translate('qwerty', to_qwerty)
'x,dokt'
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.layouts._translate_stream
documentation:
```pycon
>>> import io
>>> _translate_stream(io.StringIO('foo'), to_dvorak)
urr
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.strip-prefix.strip_prefix
documentation:
Strip any common prefix from stdin.

```pycon
>>> import io, pytest
>>> getfixture('monkeypatch').setattr('sys.stdin', io.StringIO('abcdef\nabc123'))
>>> strip_prefix()
def
123
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.show-newlines.report_newlines
documentation:
Report the newlines in the indicated file.

```pycon
>>> tmp_path = getfixture('tmp_path')
>>> filename = tmp_path / 'out.txt'
>>> _ = filename.write_text('foo\nbar\n', newline='', encoding='utf-8')
>>> report_newlines(filename)
newline is '\n'
>>> filename = tmp_path / 'out.txt'
>>> _ = filename.write_text('foo\nbar\r\n', newline='', encoding='utf-8')
>>> report_newlines(filename)
newlines are ('\n', '\r\n')
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.substitution
documentation: Return a function that will perform a substitution on a string

functions.name: jaraco.text.multi_substitution
documentation:
Take a sequence of pairs specifying substitutions, and create
a function that performs those substitutions.

```pycon
>>> multi_substitution(('foo', 'bar'), ('bar', 'baz'))('foo')
'baz'
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.is_decodable
documentation:
Return True if the supplied value is decodable (using the default
encoding).

```pycon
>>> is_decodable(b'\xff')
False
>>> is_decodable(b'\x32')
True
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.is_binary
documentation:
Return True if the value appears to be binary (that is, it's a byte
string and isn't decodable).

```pycon
>>> is_binary(b'\xff')
True
>>> is_binary('\xff')
False
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.trim
documentation:
Trim something like a docstring to remove the whitespace that
is common due to indentation and formatting.

```pycon
>>> trim("\n\tfoo = bar\n\t\tbar = baz\n")
'foo = bar\n\tbar = baz'
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.wrap
documentation:
Wrap lines of text, retaining existing newlines as
paragraph markers.

```pycon
>>> print(wrap(lorem_ipsum))
Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do
eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad
minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut
aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in
reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla
pariatur. Excepteur sint occaecat cupidatat non proident, sunt in
culpa qui officia deserunt mollit anim id est laborum.
<BLANKLINE>
Curabitur pretium tincidunt lacus. Nulla gravida orci a odio. Nullam
varius, turpis et commodo pharetra, est eros bibendum elit, nec luctus
magna felis sollicitudin mauris. Integer in mauris eu nibh euismod
gravida. Duis ac tellus et risus vulputate vehicula. Donec lobortis
risus a elit. Etiam tempor. Ut ullamcorper, ligula eu tempor congue,
eros est euismod turpis, id tincidunt sapien risus a quam. Maecenas
fermentum consequat mi. Donec fermentum. Pellentesque malesuada nulla
a mi. Duis sapien sem, aliquet nec, commodo eget, consequat quis,
neque. Aliquam faucibus, elit ut dictum aliquet, felis nisl adipiscing
sapien, sed malesuada diam lacus eget erat. Cras mollis scelerisque
nunc. Nullam arcu. Aliquam consequat. Curabitur augue lorem, dapibus
quis, laoreet et, pretium ac, nisi. Aenean magna nisl, mollis quis,
molestie eu, feugiat in, orci. In hac habitasse platea dictumst.
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.unwrap
documentation:
Given a multi-line string, return an unwrapped version.

```pycon
>>> wrapped = wrap(lorem_ipsum)
>>> wrapped.count('\n')
20
>>> unwrapped = unwrap(wrapped)
>>> unwrapped.count('\n')
1
>>> print(unwrapped)
Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing ...
Curabitur pretium tincidunt lacus. Nulla gravida orci ...
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.indent
documentation:
```pycon
>>> indent('foo')
'    foo'
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.simple_html_strip
documentation:
Remove HTML from the string `s`. ```pycon >>> str(simple_html_strip('')) '' ``` ```pycon >>> print(simple_html_strip('A stormy day in paradise')) A stormy day in paradise ``` ```pycon >>> print(simple_html_strip('Somebody tell the truth.')) Somebody tell the truth. ``` ```pycon >>> print(simple_html_strip('What about
\nmultiple lines?')) What about multiple lines? ```

functions.name: jaraco.text.remove_prefix
documentation:
Remove the prefix from the text if it exists.

```pycon
>>> remove_prefix('underwhelming performance', 'underwhelming ')
'performance'
```

```pycon
>>> remove_prefix('something special', 'sample')
'something special'
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.remove_suffix
documentation:
Remove the suffix from the text if it exists.

```pycon
>>> remove_suffix('name.git', '.git')
'name'
```

```pycon
>>> remove_suffix('something special', 'sample')
'something special'
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.normalize_newlines
documentation:
Replace alternate newlines with the canonical newline.

```pycon
>>> normalize_newlines('Lorem Ipsum\u2029')
'Lorem Ipsum\n'
>>> normalize_newlines('Lorem Ipsum\r\n')
'Lorem Ipsum\n'
>>> normalize_newlines('Lorem Ipsum\x85')
'Lorem Ipsum\n'
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.yield_lines
documentation:
Yield valid lines of a string or iterable.

```pycon
>>> list(yield_lines(''))
[]
>>> list(yield_lines(['foo', 'bar']))
['foo', 'bar']
>>> list(yield_lines('foo\nbar'))
['foo', 'bar']
>>> list(yield_lines('\nfoo\n#bar\nbaz #comment'))
['foo', 'baz #comment']
>>> list(yield_lines(['foo\nbar', 'baz', 'bing\n\n\n']))
['foo', 'bar', 'baz', 'bing']
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.clean
documentation: Yield non-blank, non-comment elements from lines.

functions.name: jaraco.text.drop_comment
documentation:
Drop comments.

```pycon
>>> drop_comment('foo # bar')
'foo'
```

A hash without a space may be in a URL.

```pycon
>>> drop_comment('http://example.com/foo#bar')
'http://example.com/foo#bar'
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.join_continuation
documentation:
Join lines continued by a trailing backslash.

```pycon
>>> list(join_continuation(['foo \\', 'bar', 'baz']))
['foobar', 'baz']
>>> list(join_continuation(['foo \\', 'bar', 'baz']))
['foobar', 'baz']
>>> list(join_continuation(['foo \\', 'bar \\', 'baz']))
['foobarbaz']
```

Not sure why, but...
The character preceding the backslash is also elided.

```pycon
>>> list(join_continuation(['goo\\', 'dly']))
['godly']
```

A terrible idea, but...
If no line is available to continue, suppress the lines.

```pycon
>>> list(join_continuation(['foo', 'bar\\', 'baz\\']))
['foo']
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.read_newlines
documentation:
```pycon
>>> tmp_path = getfixture('tmp_path')
>>> filename = tmp_path / 'out.txt'
>>> _ = filename.write_text('foo\n', newline='', encoding='utf-8')
>>> read_newlines(filename)
'\n'
>>> _ = filename.write_text('foo\r\n', newline='', encoding='utf-8')
>>> read_newlines(filename)
'\r\n'
>>> _ = filename.write_text('foo\r\nbar\nbing\r', newline='', encoding='utf-8')
>>> read_newlines(filename)
('\r', '\n', '\r\n')
```

functions.name: jaraco.text.lines_from
documentation:
Generate lines from a {class}`importlib.resources.abc.Traversable` path.

```pycon
>>> lines = lines_from(files(__name__).joinpath('Lorem ipsum.txt'))
>>> next(lines)
'Lorem ipsum...'
>>> next(lines)
'Curabitur pretium...'
```

classes.name: jaraco.text.FoldedCase

methods.name: jaraco.text.FoldedCase.casefold
documentation: cache casefold since it's likely to be called frequently.

A case insensitive string class; behaves just like str
except compares equal when the only variation is case.

```pycon
>>> s = FoldedCase('hello world')
```

```pycon
>>> s == 'Hello World'
True
```

```pycon
>>> 'Hello World' == s
True
```

```pycon
>>> s != 'Hello World'
False
```

```pycon
>>> s.index('O')
4
```

```pycon
>>> s.split('O')
['hell', ' w', 'rld']
```

```pycon
>>> sorted(map(FoldedCase, ['GAMMA', 'alpha', 'Beta']))
['alpha', 'Beta', 'GAMMA']
```

Sequence membership is straightforward.

```pycon
>>> "Hello World" in [s]
True
>>> s in ["Hello World"]
True
```

Allows testing for set inclusion, but candidate and elements
must both be folded.

```pycon
>>> FoldedCase("Hello World") in {s}
True
>>> s in {FoldedCase("Hello World")}
True
```

String inclusion works as long as the FoldedCase object
is on the right.

```pycon
>>> "hello" in FoldedCase("Hello World")
True
```

But not if the FoldedCase object is on the left:

```pycon
>>> FoldedCase('hello') in 'Hello World'
False
```

In that case, use `in_`:

```pycon
>>> FoldedCase('hello').in_('Hello World')
True
```

```pycon
>>> FoldedCase('hello') > FoldedCase('Hello')
False
```

```pycon
>>> FoldedCase('ß') == FoldedCase('ss')
True
```

classes.name: jaraco.text.Splitter
documentation:
object that will split a string with the given arguments for each call

```pycon
>>> s = Splitter(',')
>>> s('hello, world, this is your, master calling')
['hello', ' world', ' this is your', ' master calling']
```

classes.name: jaraco.text.WordSet

methods.name: jaraco.text.WordSet.trim_right
documentation:
Remove the item from the end of the set.

```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse('foo bar').trim_right('foo')
('foo', 'bar')
>>> WordSet.parse('foo bar').trim_right('bar')
('foo',)
>>> WordSet.parse('').trim_right('bar')
()
```

methods.name: jaraco.text.WordSet.trim_left
documentation:
Remove the item from the beginning of the set.

```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse('foo bar').trim_left('foo')
('bar',)
>>> WordSet.parse('foo bar').trim_left('bar')
('foo', 'bar')
>>> WordSet.parse('').trim_left('bar')
()
```

methods.name: jaraco.text.WordSet.trim
documentation:
```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse('foo bar').trim('foo')
('bar',)
```

Given an identifier, return the words that identifier represents,
whether in camel case, underscore-separated, etc.

```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse("camelCase")
('camel', 'Case')
```

```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse("under_sep")
('under', 'sep')
```

Acronyms should be retained

```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse("firstSNL")
('first', 'SNL')
```

```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse("you_and_I")
('you', 'and', 'I')
```

```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse("A simple test")
('A', 'simple', 'test')
```

Multiple caps should not interfere with the first cap of another word.

```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse("myABCClass")
('my', 'ABC', 'Class')
```

The result is a WordSet, providing access to other forms.

```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse("myABCClass").underscore_separated()
'my_ABC_Class'
```

```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse('a-command').camel_case()
'ACommand'
```

```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse('someIdentifier').lowered().space_separated()
'some identifier'
```

Slices of the result should return another WordSet.

```pycon
>>> WordSet.parse('taken-out-of-context')[1:].underscore_separated()
'out_of_context'
```

```pycon
>>> WordSet.from_class_name(WordSet()).lowered().space_separated()
'word set'
```

```pycon
>>> example = WordSet.parse('figured it out')
>>> example.headless_camel_case()
'figuredItOut'
>>> example.dash_separated()
'figured-it-out'
```

classes.name: jaraco.text.SeparatedValues
documentation:
A string separated by a separator. Overrides \_\_iter\_\_ for getting
the values.

```pycon
>>> list(SeparatedValues('a,b,c'))
['a', 'b', 'c']
```

Whitespace is stripped and empty values are discarded.

```pycon
>>> list(SeparatedValues(' a,   b   , c,  '))
['a', 'b', 'c']
```

classes.name: jaraco.text.Stripper

methods.name: jaraco.text.Stripper.common_prefix
documentation: Return the common prefix of two lines.

Given a series of lines, find the common prefix and strip it from them.

```pycon
>>> lines = [
...     'abcdefg\n',
...     'abc\n',
...     'abcde\n',
... ]
>>> res = Stripper.strip_prefix(lines)
>>> res.prefix
'abc'
>>> list(res.lines)
['defg\n', '\n', 'de\n']
```

If no prefix is common, nothing should be stripped.

```pycon
>>> lines = [
...     'abcd\n',
...     '1234\n',
... ]
>>> res = Stripper.strip_prefix(lines)
>>> res.prefix = ''
>>> list(res.lines)
['abcd\n', '1234\n']
```
